Believe it or not, everyone in this room added encouragement to her by not being there. She had gone this far and somewhere in her being she didn't want to let anybody down. She wanted to do this. People had put their lives out for us. We couldn't have been here there for that stretch if it wasn't for people in this room sacrificing and compromising and enabling us to even be there. And I know that that was ringing in her head and I know that, listen, Marie had done all the work. She did it all seven nights on Skid Row. I kept telling her, I don't know another woman that would do that with her husband. I couldn't even think of another person. I challenged you to do the same, seven nights on Skid Row. I said, you've done all the work. I go, let's go get a reward. Let's go sit. The people in that room, all the people that we met, we didn't meet them. They knew us and they saw we were not kidding around. I mean, we lived there. It wasn't a show, it wasn't a game. We lived there. We interacted with them. From the crazies, we had nicknames for people. I mean, really, I'm not kidding. We had nicknames, people called us Mr. Mrs. Smith. They called us Hollywood. They called us the movie stars. They called us talent. Hershey's just seen the big silver cross he had hanging down his chest and a do-rag on his head. Towards the end of the week, we had actually made a call to one of the folks on your staff and he had encouraged us to have some fun now. You guys, it sounds like you have conquered everything. Have some fun. Be creative. We were able to come up with a couple of dollars and we went down to one of the local stores and picked up a do-rag and some cheap sunglasses and a cheap cross. We started singing songs in the street just to kind of fit in and singing and carrying on. I started doing that when he was getting the laugh out of it. So I would just do it more and more because it was kind of bringing some levity and taking some pressure off. One day we walked into the courtyard. I was just singing with my old boy Garbond and just having fun and making fun. People looking at us like, what the hell? He lost it. He lost it. He's the earliest place he's got to. But somehow when we just came up with the muster to say, I'll do it but I'm not talking. I'm not going to say anything. I'm not talking. I said, Marie, we're going to be with people that you know. I said, you may get to a point where they ask a question and then maybe you can just ask a pointed question. I said, if you don't feel like doing that, I'll answer for you. I said, at least let's show up and kind of just walk across the finish line. So I went in. Of course he went first. You go first and I just went in. Meanwhile, I mentioned these guys earlier when we began. I said, off camera I said, things you're going to go do. I just want you to know, I don't have empathy for. I've experienced these things. I've flown the Big Jet. I've done all these different things in my life. And so the word I heard was they're pissed because you've never been homeless and I was. And I slept on top of the hill with nothing. I went down and I slept in the laundry room of a family member or friends rather. And just lived in that place for a couple of weeks. Then I lived literally in a 1968 Volkswagen for a while and my trench coat parked at 7-11 because it was 24 hours. So I knew what the experience was like. And I knew that from it, it gave me a place I'll never, ever forget. It makes me grateful. It makes me care about other strangers. It makes me never go back there. And it also gives me contrast to any other problem I ever have. So I knew it's there. So I shared that with them. And then Ron came in and I let everybody know what's happening and introduced them, came in and he sat down. And the first thing he said, down, said, Now I know you got your plan here and your strategy. He said, let me just tell you how it is. I've had it. You're wrong. You don't know what this feels like. You've never done. You said you've done what we've done. You've not done what it, and part of me was like, what the hell? And then I'm trying not to smile because I'm going to look at this man show up. You know, just totally taking control of the situation. It was really, really exciting. So I listened to him rant at me for a good three or four minutes, right? And I said, no, you're wrong. I have had the experience. It's different than your experience. Because you haven't had your wife down here. And I said, that wasn't my lesson. I didn't need to learn the lesson to put my wife first. So you had a different experience. And I said, so I get it. And he said, what have you learned? He said, you know, I have to put her first. I'm going to describe all the things he was telling you. He told me, and I'm just trying not to grim from ear to ear. And finally I just reached over and said, brother, that is what this was all about. And then I asked, why are you out here since you don't want to talk and you're not going to do it and everything else? And what did you share with me? It was beautiful. Why did you come out? I came out because I didn't want to let him down. I didn't want to let you guys down. And I just wanted to thank everyone that was in the room that we were friends with. I said, I couldn't do it without anybody. But everyone helped us so much. It was like the best experience for me. It's kind of funny saying that. And so both these people got out of themselves. He did it to take care of her. She did it to take care of him. Direct opposite. This is what everything that we're working for is for us. That's concern. But then Ron got very intense. And we're from New York. We don't need more than three days. We're not stupid. We learned quickly. We were done three days ago. And I said to him, just like he said to you here, and I said, you know, that's not true. I said, it's not true because seven days is really nice. I said, look at these people here. And I pointed to all the people that are living there. I said, they don't have the privilege of saying, I'm done after three days. I got the lesson. They even got the lesson two years ago. The lesson you guys hadn't gotten was endurance. You had not gotten endurance. And you got a lot more of it now. Like light years more of it now. If we'd stopped on the third day and you hadn't done those other three, four days of it, there's no way in hell you'd have the same strength you have right now. True or false? Yeah, totally true. You know, and they talked about how when they lived on the street, one of the big questions they asked you is, what was on the street versus inside? What did you tell them about that? When you're living on the street versus when you're inside serving people? Well, we went from survival mode to two-servant mode. I mean, that's pretty much we were either in one mode or the other the entire time. You know, constantly looking over your shoulder, you know, wondering, you know, if somebody's going to trip with some needle in their hand. And we were seeing people going to bathrooms and coming out all cracked out. Totally. So it was right around us, as close as you are to us. So, you know, it was survival because obviously it had to protect us from that. And then when we were inside, I mean, we were serving homeless people, people that had nothing. So we were constantly going from one meeting to the other. Which was a perfect lesson in life, right? Everybody, we're always in survival when we're focusing ourselves. And the beauty of family is, you know, something about family makes you get outside yourself because you love somebody more than yourself and you're trying to serve them. And it was beautiful. And they talked about how happy everybody was there. Amazing. Just amazing. People, I think the majority of the people we bonded with at the highest level, the ones that were homeless and then had enrolled in this program to get help. Right. And there's a lot of help for people that are without. There just is. There's a lot of government programs. I couldn't even tell you where the nearest one to us is, but I will know in the next day or two. I'm going to reach out and find out what's available and see if we can do some volunteering because that's how much we were touched. But the people that were in the program actually helping themselves, realize where they came from, realizing what the potential is to their lives, if they stay clean and stay focused on what they can do. We just bonded with them at high levels. So at the point where we have probably a dozen or so names and numbers of people that we're going to keep in touch with probably forever. And like you said before, I mean these people didn't even have to come up to us and every single person came. It was a men's facility too. So it was obviously all men. I mean, they just gave the whole time. You know, it just brought an awareness to us that we weren't really cognizant of that we're going to have to do more of. The feeling you get from giving of yourself and giving out of the lifestyle that we have to people that don't have is just immeasurable. Notice, you know, he's remembering the lifestyle we have even in a mitigated version of your lifestyle. Two thirds of the planet lives on two dollars a day. You know, we forget that. So at the end, after the people asked all these questions and they interrogated Ron about giving away too much chicken. I got reprimand if I give him too much chicken to the homeless. If you do it, you're going to stand up saying you believe in them. If you don't remain seated, if you all stand up, they go home. If not, they stay another week. There was this long breath over here or should say lack of breath and everybody stood up and we did not coach them. Every single person stood up and clapped for them and cheered for them and they made their trip home. One symbolism that I'll never forget. You know how much she was crying when she came there? She was crying almost as much when it was time to go. Because of all the people that we met and the relationships we had built and that seven night stretch that were very special to us. And really we couldn't have done it without some of the people that were there. So she was crying as much as she could leave it as she was coming in but for a different reason of course. So there's, you know, I knew coming back that they had transformed. They had focused on each other. They had gotten out of themselves. They had faced fears. They had faced pressures. They had found a way to nurture each other. They had found a way to give. They found a way to find some joy in the middle of what would have been misery. They had some contrast for their life. So the next piece is to come back and take care of family. You know, they'd unified. Now it's time to come back and unify the family which is why I brought them here. I know we've been sitting here for a while, but would you share a little bit about what's happened here today for both of you? Either one of you can share. When we heard there would be a meeting of sorts here today, immediately my mind went to, I wonder if my parents will be here. Mine too. You know, we knew that that was a part of our life that was an open wound. We haven't spoken in the three, four plus years. It was really my decision to sort of cut ties with my parents. We had undergone some arguments. And the other issues would cloud it, and you'd not see it because you see the things that are in front of you right now.
